title: On the Riemann hypothesis: a proof via fine structure of the explicit formula
abstract: We prove the Riemann hypothesis by extracting an unconditional positivity bound from a refined version of Weil's explicit formula.
ai_proof_review:
## Setup

The argument tries to upgrade the well-known positivity criterion (RH is equivalent to non-negativity of a particular quadratic form on test functions) by inserting a new family of test functions said to make the criterion verifiable.

## Section 3 (the new test functions)

The construction is concrete and the formulae are correct as stated. So far so good.

## Lemma 5.1 — the critical step

The lemma claims that a certain double sum is bounded by a single sum of the same shape. The proof invokes a 'standard rearrangement' but the rearrangement implicitly assumes the absolute convergence of an intermediate series that, as far as I can tell, is **conditionally** convergent at best. Without uniform absolute control, the swap of summation order is unjustified. The entire positivity bound rests on this lemma.

## Overall

The surrounding framework is competent and the test-function construction is genuinely interesting. But Lemma 5.1 is the load-bearing step and the proof, as written, does not close it. If the gap can be patched by a more careful absolute-convergence argument, the paper is plausible; if not, the whole structure collapses. Strong prior says such a one-page proof of RH would have been spotted earlier — the burden of proof is heavy and unmet.
